original_abstract:
Liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (LC-MS) is a cornerstone of modern metabolomics, enabling the rapid quantification of cellular metabolites based on their mass-to-charge ratios and chromatographic retention times. With the advent of high-resolution, fast-scanning mass spectrometers, there has been a growing trend toward high-throughput screening using minimal or even no chromatographic separation. However, when analyzing complex biological extracts, the absence of prior separation can lead to the serious misannotation of metabolites. 

In this study, we demonstrate that even electrospray ionization—widely considered a "soft" ionization technique—produces significant in-source fragmentation. These unintended byproducts often possess the same exact mass as other common metabolites. For instance, we show that nucleotide-triphosphates frequently fragment into nucleotide-diphosphates, while hexose-phosphates can degrade into triose-phosphates within the ion source. By evaluating yeast intracellular extracts, we identified over 20 instances where in-source fragments effectively "mimic" endogenous metabolites, potentially leading to substantial errors in quantification. Our findings underscore that robust chromatographic separation remains essential for the accurate identification and measurement of many central cellular metabolites.
humanized_abstract:
Liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (LC-MS) is currently the major analytical technique in metabolomics. Thousands of metabolites in cells can be quantified in a short time based on their mass-to-charge ratio and retention time. Due to the rapid development of high-resolution and fast-scanning mass spectrometers, it is now feasible to perform high-throughput untargeted mass spectrometry with minimal or even no chromatography. Although chromatography can effectively improve analytical selectivity and accuracy, it is still necessary to retain chromatography prior to mass spectrometry when dealing with complex samples such as bio-extracts.

In this study, we demonstrate for the first time that even a soft ionization source such as ESI leads to considerable in-source fragmentation. Fragment ions can correspond to real endogenous metabolites. For example, nucleotide-triphosphates are frequently fragmented to their corresponding nucleotide-diphosphates in the source, and hexose-phosphates are fragmented into triose-phosphates. Using yeast intracellular extracts, we give 20 examples of source fragmentation products that can masquerade as the endogenous metabolites of interest and, therefore, lead to potential quantification inaccuracies. In any case, our study confirms the importance of good chromatographic resolution for the precise determination of many central metabolic compounds.

Although chromatography can effectively improve analytical selectivity and accuracy, it is still necessary to retain chromatography prior to mass spectrometry when dealing with complex samples such as bio-extracts.

In this study, we demonstrate for the first time that even a soft ionization source such as ESI leads to considerable in-source fragmentation.
